% =============================================================
%  Sections added by the accelerometer-based prediction work.
%  Include from main.tex with \input{prediction_sections}.
%  Numerical values come from prediction/results_macros.tex which is
%  regenerated by scripts/build_prediction_report_assets.py.
% =============================================================

\section{Height-Difference Prediction}
\label{sec:height-prediction}

This section introduces the two accelerometer-only algorithms that, given
a single pre-segmented elevator ride, return an estimate of the vertical
distance travelled together with an empirically-calibrated $90\%$
confidence interval and a quality verdict. Both algorithms are
registered as first-class members of the \texttt{PredictAlgorithm} enum
under \texttt{src/prediction/algorithms/} and instantiated through the
\texttt{Predictor} dispatcher (\S\ref{sec:predictor-api}), so that the
downstream inference code is algorithm-agnostic.

\subsection{Predictor API}
\label{sec:predictor-api}

Every algorithm implements three public methods (the signatures live in
\texttt{\small src/prediction/\allowbreak{}algorithms/\allowbreak{}predictor.py}):

\begin{itemize}
  \item \texttt{predict(data, phone\_model, pre, post)} $\;\to\;$
        \texttt{PredictionOutput} with fields
        \{\texttt{height\_diff, ci\_half\_width, theoretical\_sigma,
        accepted, quality\_score, reject\_reason, meta}\}. The
        \texttt{PredictionOutput} dataclass is defined in
        \texttt{\small src/prediction/\allowbreak{}algorithms/\allowbreak{}common/\allowbreak{}types.py}.
  \item \texttt{forward(data)} $\;\to\;$ \texttt{float}, the legacy
        float-in-float-out path used by the barometer baseline and
        external code.
  \item \texttt{calibrate(samples)} — fits the conformal multiplier on
        a set of labelled train samples and persists the checkpoint
        next to the calibration JSON. No-op for the barometer
        baseline.
\end{itemize}

Both accelerometer algorithms share a lightweight \texttt{common/}
package that provides: a phone-model $\to$ sensor-chip $\to$ noise
lookup (\texttt{common/noise\_db.py}), a split-conformal calibrator
(\texttt{common/conformal.py}), a set of vector helpers (gravity
estimation, vertical projection, ZUPT integration), and the
shared-shape trapezoid pulse-pair fitter
(\texttt{common/pulse\_pair.py}) that Algorithm~B is built on.
Everything else — quality filter, theoretical-$\sigma$ model,
amplitude correction — is per-algorithm.

\subsection{Algorithm A: ZUPT Double-Integration}
\label{sec:algo-zupt}

Zero-Velocity Update (ZUPT) double-integration exploits the two facts
highlighted in \S\ref{sec:elevator-kinematics}: the cabin starts and
ends at rest, so in the absence of sensor noise the velocity
$v(t)=\int_0^t a(\tau)\,d\tau$ must satisfy $v(0)=v(T)=0$. We enforce
this boundary condition explicitly and apply a linear drift correction
to absorb the slow-varying bias that the static-bias model of the
accelerometer cannot cancel:
\begin{equation}
  v(t) = \int_0^t a(\tau)\,d\tau \;-\; \frac{t}{T}\,\int_0^T a(\tau)\,d\tau,
  \qquad
  \Delta h = \int_0^T v(t)\,dt.
  \label{eq:zupt-corr}
\end{equation}
The algorithm operates on the vertical component of acceleration,
$a_\mathrm{vert}(t)$, obtained by one of three strategies, chosen
automatically per segment based on pre/post-ride gravity stability
(the reasoning behind the cascade is in \texttt{zupt\_accel/estimator.py}):
\begin{enumerate}
  \item[(i)]   \emph{Pre+post projection.} When both pre- and post-ride
               stationary windows have $|{\bf g}|\in[8,12]$~m/s$^2$ and
               window-to-window standard deviation below a threshold,
               we compute a weighted mean gravity vector
               $\hat{\bf g}=(w_1{\bf g}_{\rm pre}+w_2{\bf g}_{\rm post})/(w_1+w_2)$
               with weights $w_i=1/\sigma_i$, and project
               $a_\mathrm{vert}=\mathbf{a}\cdot\hat{\bf g}/|\hat{\bf g}|-|\hat{\bf g}|$.
  \item[(ii)]  \emph{One-sided projection.} If only one of the windows
               is stable, its gravity vector is used.
  \item[(iii)] \emph{Magnitude fallback.}
               $a_\mathrm{vert}=|{\bf a}|-\bar{|{\bf a}|}$. This is
               rotation-invariant but loses sign information, so the
               direction is recovered from the sign of the first
               acceleration pulse.
\end{enumerate}
The active-motion window $[t_s,t_e]$ is located by thresholding the
moving-average of $|a_\mathrm{vert}|$ at $0.1$~m/s$^2$; acceleration
outside the window is zeroed before integration. This prevents
long pre/post stationary periods from inflating $\int a\,dt$ with
noise-driven drift. The number of samples inside the window,
$N=t_e-t_s+1$, is the key scale for the theoretical error analysis
below (\S\ref{sec:theoretical-sigma}).

\subsection{Algorithm B: Shared-Shape Trapezoid Pulse Pair}
\label{sec:algo-trapezoid}

We rely on the kinematic derivation of an elevator ride given in
\S\ref{sec:elevator-kinematics} (equations
\eqref{eq:elevator-bounds}--\eqref{eq:elevator-template}) and the
trapezoid-kernel parameterisation $\tau_{W,f}(t)$ introduced by the
detector in \eqref{eq:trap-template}. Prediction re-uses the same
two shape parameters $(W,f)$ — there is no second derivation of the
pulse geometry. The material below only specifies what prediction
adds on top of the shared kinematics: the shared-shape constraint
between the two lobes of a ride, the analytic $\Delta h$ formula,
and the matched-filter grid search that produces the fit.

The seven-phase S-curve acceleration template of
\S\ref{sec:elevator-kinematics} is the ideal jerk-limited profile,
but fitting it in the velocity domain with three free kinematic
parameters $(j_{\max},a_{\max},v_{\max})$ and non-linear least squares
brought two problems on our field data: (i)~on long rides the NLS
optimiser walked into local minima produced by non-ideal mid-ride
events (door bounces, floor-passing vibrations), and (ii)~the
jerk-ramp phases occupy a small fraction of the ride duration, so
the information content about $j_{\max}$ separate from $a_{\max}$
is weak under realistic sensor noise. We therefore collapse the
jerk-ramp phases into a single \emph{trapezoid pulse} — still a jerk-
limited profile in the limit where $j_{\max}$ is large compared to
$a_{\max}/T_\mathrm{lobe}$, but parameterised by the shape features
that are actually identifiable from the data.

\paragraph{The 2-shape-parameter lobe model.} Each acceleration lobe
(take-off and landing) is modelled as the symmetric trapezoid
template $\tau_{W,f}(t)$ defined in \eqref{eq:trap-template} of the
Segmentation \& Detection chapter — half-width $W$, flat-fraction
$f\in[0,1]$, total lobe width $2W$, flat top $2fW$, linear ramps of
width $(1-f)W$. We re-use the same kernel here rather than
redefining it, since the shape is identified with the
jerk-limited ramp-plateau structure of the ride
(\S\ref{sec:elevator-kinematics}) in both places. Only two shape
parameters; the limits $f{=}0$ and $f{=}1$ recover the triangular and
rectangular pulses respectively. The integral of the unit trapezoid,
\begin{equation}
  \int_{-W}^{W}\tau_{W,f}(t)\,dt \;=\; W(1+f),
  \label{eq:trapezoid-area}
\end{equation}
is the bridge from shape to velocity that the detector does not need
but the prediction \emph{does} (it turns a fit into an analytic
$\Delta h$ below).

\paragraph{Shared-shape constraint.} A real elevator returns to rest,
so
\[
  \int_{0}^{T}\!a(\tau)\,d\tau \;=\; 0 \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
  \int\!\!\bigl(\text{accel lobe}\bigr)
  \;=\;
  -\int\!\!\bigl(\text{decel lobe}\bigr),
\]
and under the jerk-limited assumption the two lobes also share the
machine-level limits $(j_{\max},a_{\max})$, hence the same
$(A,W,f)$. We therefore fit the ride as
\begin{equation}
  a_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(t) \;=\;
  s\,A\,\tau_{W,f}(t-t_{c1})
  \;-\;
  s\,A\,\tau_{W,f}(t-t_{c2})\,,
  \qquad
  \boldsymbol{\theta}=(A,W,f,t_{c1},t_{c2})\in\mathbb{R}_+^{3}\times\mathbb{R}^2,
  \label{eq:pulse-pair}
\end{equation}
where $s\in\{+1,-1\}$ is the ride direction (picked by the search)
and the amplitude $A\ge0$ is shared. This drops the effective degrees
of freedom from $8$ (independent per-lobe) to $5$, removing exactly
the freedom that previously overfit noise.

\paragraph{Analytic height.} The velocity profile is the integral of
\eqref{eq:pulse-pair}: zero outside the lobes, ramps up over
$[t_{c1}-W,\,t_{c1}+W]$, cruises at $v_\mathrm{peak}$ between the
lobes, ramps down over $[t_{c2}-W,\,t_{c2}+W]$. The integral
telescopes, giving
\begin{equation}
  v_\mathrm{peak} \;=\; A\,W(1+f)\,,
  \qquad
  \boxed{\;\Delta h \;=\; s \cdot v_\mathrm{peak}\cdot(t_{c2}-t_{c1})
  \;=\; s\,A\,W(1+f)\,\Delta t_c\;}\,.
  \label{eq:dh-analytic}
\end{equation}
No numerical integration, no free parameter fits outside the
matched-filter step below.

\paragraph{Matched-filter grid search (reusing Eyal's primitive).}
For a fixed $(W,f)$ the best amplitude at every candidate centre $i$
is the closed-form LS solution
\begin{equation}
  \hat{A}(W,f,i) \;=\; \frac{\langle a,\,\tau_i\rangle}{\langle \tau_i,\,\tau_i\rangle},
  \qquad
  R^2_i \;=\; 1-\frac{\lVert a-\hat{A}\tau_i\rVert^2}{\lVert a\rVert^2}\,,
  \label{eq:matched-filter}
\end{equation}
where $\tau_i(t)\equiv\tau_{W,f}(t-t_i)$ and the inner products are
taken over the $\pm W$ window around $i$. The joint shared-shape
optimum for a $(+,-)$ lobe pair in the ride window is also
closed-form (Eyal's \S\ref{sec:segmentation-detection}, equation
repeated here):
\begin{equation}
  |\hat{A}^{\star}|(W,f,i_1,i_2)
  \;=\;
  \frac{s\langle a,\tau_{i_1}\rangle -s\langle a,\tau_{i_2}\rangle}
       {2\,\langle\tau,\tau\rangle}\,,
  \qquad
  \text{score} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl(R^2_{i_1}+R^2_{i_2}\bigr).
  \label{eq:constrained-matched-filter}
\end{equation}
We exhaust the discrete $(W,f)$ grid —
$30\!\times\!15=450$ shapes, with $W\in[W_{\min}(T),\,3.0]$~s and
$f\in[0,0.8]$. A \emph{duration-adaptive} floor
$W_{\min}(T)=\max(0.3,\,\alpha_W\,T)$ prevents the matched filter from
latching onto narrow noise spikes on long rides: on a 33~s
$\sim40$~m ride a 0.5~s-wide template has higher local $R^2$ than
the physical 2~s lobe, because of sub-second hand-tremor spikes; the
adaptive floor excludes those templates from contention. We use
$\alpha_W=0.04$ in line with observed accel-phase / ride-duration
ratios in our dataset.

\paragraph{Velocity-anchored amplitude.} Even with an adaptive $W$
floor, the matched filter is biased toward narrow templates whenever
noise spikes are present, because local $R^2$ rewards close fits on
small windows. To make the amplitude robust to this, we \emph{anchor}
$A$ to the measured cruise velocity: after the shape fit, we rescale
\begin{equation}
  \widetilde{A} \;=\; \frac{|\bar v_\mathrm{cruise}|}{W(1+f)}\,,
  \qquad
  \bar v_\mathrm{cruise}
  \;=\;
  \frac{1}{|\mathcal{C}|}\sum_{t\in\mathcal{C}} v(t)\,,
  \qquad
  \mathcal{C} = (t_{c1}{+}W,\,t_{c2}{-}W)\,,
  \label{eq:velocity-anchor}
\end{equation}
where $v(t)$ is the ZUPT-integrated (drift-corrected) velocity inside
the cruise window $\mathcal{C}$. Intuitively: the shape fit tells us
\emph{where} the lobes are; the integrated velocity tells us \emph{how
fast} the cabin is moving during cruise. Combining these,
\begin{equation}
  \widehat{\Delta h} \;=\; s\,\widetilde{A}\,W(1+f)\,\Delta t_c
  \;=\; s\,\bar v_\mathrm{cruise}\,\Delta t_c\,,
  \label{eq:dh-anchored}
\end{equation}
so the centre-to-centre spacing $\Delta t_c$ from the shape fit
multiplies the cruise velocity measured directly from ZUPT integration.
Rides with no genuine cruise window (triangular short rides with
$|\mathcal{C}| < 0.5$~s) fall back to the unanchored
\eqref{eq:dh-analytic}.

\paragraph{Why not DTW?} An earlier README called this algorithm
``DTW template matching''; it is not. Dynamic time warping aligns a
template to a signal under a non-linear time reparametrisation,
which is useful when the template morphology is distorted along the
time axis. For a jerk-limited elevator pulse the morphology
\emph{is} a scaled/stretched trapezoid — no warping is needed beyond
the two-parameter $(W,f)$ scaling that the matched-filter grid
already performs. Adding a DTW layer on top would (a) cost
$\mathcal O(N^2)$ per template instead of $\mathcal O(N\log N)$ via
FFT, inflating total runtime by a factor of order $10\times N_W\times
N_f$, and (b) offer no new degrees of freedom that are physically
meaningful, since in-ride time warping is ruled out by the
jerk-limited model. We therefore stay with the NCC matched filter.

\subsection{Short-Ride Extension: Joined-Pulse Fit and ZUPT Fallback}
\label{sec:algo-joined}

The pair-fit model \eqref{eq:pulse-pair} assumes the two acceleration
lobes are separated by a cruise window of positive duration. That
assumption breaks down for short floor-to-floor rides where the
cabin never reaches its $v_{\max}$: the velocity profile is
\emph{triangular} rather than trapezoidal, the cruise window has
zero duration, and the two acceleration lobes sit immediately
adjacent to one another with $\Delta t_c = 2W$. In the
pair-fit parameterisation the spacing becomes un-identifiable up to
an exchange $(t_{c1},t_{c2})\leftrightarrow(t_{c2},t_{c1})$ shifted
by a fraction of a sample, and the matched-filter CRB we derived
for $\sigma_{t_c}^2$ no longer describes the true Fisher
information because the off-diagonal block starts contributing.
In data this shows up as an empirical coverage hole in the
$|\Delta h|\!\in\![0,3]$~m bin (Figure~\ref{fig:train-cov-bins}):
either the model silently fits a spurious narrow-cruise solution
with under-counted $\sigma$, or the quality filter rejects the
segment even though the underlying ride was a legitimate short hop.

\begin{figure}[H]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{triangle_vs_trapezoid.png}
  \caption{The two regimes side by side, both real rides from the
  training set. \textbf{Left.} A short ride — the positive and
  negative lobes meet at the peak-velocity instant, no cruise.
  This is the joined-pulse regime $\Delta t_c = 2W$: four free
  parameters $(A, W, f, t_\mathrm{mid})$, Fisher information is
  diagonal in the remaining three, $\Delta h = 2sAW^2(1+f)$.
  \textbf{Right.} A long ride with a clearly separated cruise at
  $a\!\approx\!0$ between the two lobes. Five free parameters,
  $\Delta t_c \gg 2W$, $\Delta h = sAW(1+f)\Delta t_c$. The two
  models are the two regimes of the same physics; the estimator
  runs both and picks by joint $R^2$.}
  \label{fig:triangle-vs-trapezoid}
\end{figure}

\paragraph{Research question.} Instead of rejecting these short
rides wholesale — the easy, wrong answer — we want an extension to
the pair-fit that (i) explicitly models the triangular-velocity
regime, (ii) produces a usable $\Delta h$ estimate and honest
$\sigma$ for it, and (iii) gracefully degrades when even that fails.
We considered four candidates and empirically compared them on the
train split:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Constrained joined-pulse fit.} Keep the trapezoid
        kernel but force $\Delta t_c = 2W$, reducing the model to
        four free parameters $(A, W, f, t_\mathrm{mid})$ with one
        analytic constraint. The matched-filter target is a single
        bipolar template $\tau_{W,f}(t-t_\mathrm{mid}+W) -
        \tau_{W,f}(t-t_\mathrm{mid}-W)$ of total width $4W$.
  \item \textbf{Single-lobe interpretation.} Treat the trace as a
        single symmetric pulse and recover $\Delta h$ from the lobe
        integral. Equivalent in closed form but empirically worse
        because it discards the sign information that distinguishes
        up and down rides.
  \item \textbf{ZUPT fallback.} When both pair and joined fits have
        $R^2 < \tau_\mathrm{zupt}$ (we set $\tau_\mathrm{zupt}=0.20$),
        report the integrated-velocity displacement instead. On a
        short ride ZUPT's cumulative drift is small; on a longer
        ride-that-wasn't-really-a-ride it at least returns a sane
        number with a wide CI.
  \item \textbf{Keep-reject policy.} Leave the estimator unchanged
        and harden the quality filter's W-relative overlap reject.
        This was the behaviour after the earlier CI overhaul and
        the one we explicitly reject here — it throws away valid
        data the physics says we \emph{can} model.
\end{enumerate}

\paragraph{Decision logic we ship.} We run both the pair fit and
the joined-pulse fit on every segment and pick between them on
three criteria:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item If only one fit converges, use that one.
  \item If the joined fit has a higher joint $R^2$ by more than
        $\varepsilon_{R^2}=0.02$, use the joined fit.
  \item If the pair fit has $\Delta t_c/2W < \beta_\mathrm{overlap}=1.15$
        (overlap-regime warning) and the joined fit is within
        $\varepsilon_{R^2}$ of the pair $R^2$, prefer joined
        (Occam's razor: same $R^2$, one fewer free parameter).
  \item Otherwise keep the pair fit.
\end{enumerate}
If both fits have $R^2 < \tau_\mathrm{zupt}$ (or both fail to
converge), the estimator emits an inline ZUPT double-integration
result with \texttt{reject\_reason="zupt\_fallback\_both\_fits\_failed"},
so the segment is recorded but not trusted for conformal
calibration.

\paragraph{Analytic height in the joined regime.} Integrating the
joined template gives
\begin{equation}
  \boxed{\;\Delta h \;=\; 2\,s\,A\,W^{2}(1+f)\;}\,,
  \label{eq:dh-joined}
\end{equation}
which reproduces \eqref{eq:dh-analytic} with $\Delta t_c$ set to
$2W$: the bipolar pulse has peak velocity $v_\mathrm{peak}=
A\,W(1+f)$ at $t=t_\mathrm{mid}$, and the triangular velocity
profile integrates to $v_\mathrm{peak}\cdot 2W$. The delta-method
propagation through \eqref{eq:dh-joined} uses three gradients
\begin{equation}
  \frac{\partial\Delta h}{\partial A} = 2sW^{2}(1+f),\quad
  \frac{\partial\Delta h}{\partial W} = 4sAW(1+f),\quad
  \frac{\partial\Delta h}{\partial f} = 2sAW^{2},
  \label{eq:dh-joined-gradients}
\end{equation}
since $\Delta h$ is invariant under a shift of $t_\mathrm{mid}$,
i.e.\ $\partial\Delta h/\partial t_\mathrm{mid}=0$ — the time-of-
arrival degree of freedom contributes exactly zero to the height
uncertainty. The matched-filter CRB on $A$ uses the joined-template
norm
\(\langle\tau_\mathrm{joined},\tau_\mathrm{joined}\rangle =
2\langle\tau,\tau\rangle\), which is a factor of two smaller than
for the single-lobe matched filter and reflects the fact that two
touching lobes pack twice the template energy under the same noise.

\paragraph{Why this beats the alternatives.}
\begin{itemize}
  \item Candidate 2 (single-lobe) loses the up/down direction
        signal and was discarded. Candidate 4 (hard reject) fails
        the research goal of \emph{fitting} short rides, not
        discarding them.
  \item Candidate 1 (joined fit) has one fewer free parameter than
        the pair fit. In the triangular-velocity regime where
        $\Delta t_c \equiv 2W$, that extra degree of freedom in the
        pair fit can only fit noise. Removing it is a bias-variance
        win: the pair fit's CI widens faster than the joined fit's
        as fit residuals grow, because the pair has more gradient
        directions along which to spread the same uncertainty.
  \item Candidate 3 (ZUPT fallback) is the right floor-of-last-
        resort because ZUPT's error is bounded by
        $\sigma_a \Delta t^{2}\sqrt{N^{3}/12}$, which for a short
        ride ($N\lesssim 200$ at 50~Hz) stays well below 1~m. On
        longer rides the drift-free joined fit is the better
        choice, so the fallback only fires when both matched-filter
        models genuinely disagree with the data.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Cost.} The joined-pulse fit uses the same
$(W,f)$ grid as the pair fit (30$\times$15 templates) and a single
centre-shift search per template, so each run is comparable in cost
to the pair search. Running both in parallel roughly doubles the
per-segment fitting time but stays well under 1~s per ride on our
hardware, and the increase is paid only once per segment — the
conformal calibration, quality filter, and CI construction
downstream are unchanged.

\subsection{Quality Filter}
\label{sec:quality-filter}

Both algorithms go through a per-segment quality filter before their
estimate is reported. The filter uses \emph{accelerometer-only}
features (no barometer, no orientation fusion) so the pipeline remains
independent of secondary sensors. Each segment is scored on a small
number of physically interpretable features (full list in
\texttt{zupt\_accel/quality.py} and \texttt{trapezoid\_accel/quality.py}):
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Pre/post gravity magnitude} and \textbf{window
        stability} — the phone should rest between $[8,12]$~m/s$^2$
        with intra-window $\sigma_{|{\bf g}|}\lesssim 1$~m/s$^2$.
  \item \textbf{Pre-vs-post gravity angle} — large rotation of the
        phone body frame breaks the gravity-projection step.
  \item \textbf{During-ride gravity drift} — max angle swept across
        1-s chunks; bounded by $15^\circ$ before we reject.
  \item \textbf{Max transient peak} — impacts above $8$~m/s$^2$
        dominate the integral.
  \item \textbf{Active-motion fraction} — fraction of samples with
        $|a_\mathrm{vert}|>0.1$~m/s$^2$; very low fractions on long
        segments indicate a non-ride.
  \item \textbf{Fit joint $R^2$} (trapezoid only) — the
        shared-shape matched-filter score of the $(+,-)$ lobe pair.
        Below $0.45$ the ride is flagged as having no clear pulse
        structure, e.g.\ when there is no real cabin motion or the
        vertical projection is badly misaligned.
  \item \textbf{Lobe spacing} $\Delta t_c$ (trapezoid only) —
        spacings below $0.8$~s are too tight to be a physical ride.
  \item \textbf{Residual autocorrelation} (trapezoid only) — a
        residual-ACF(1) bigger than $0.6$ indicates strong coloured
        noise the model hasn't captured.
\end{itemize}
The features are combined into a continuous score in $[0,10]$ via
weights tuned on the training set; on top of the score, each feature
also has a hard threshold that triggers an outright rejection with a
human-readable reason (e.g.\ \texttt{"ride\_drift\_22deg"}). The
quality filter is a \emph{filter}, not a predictor: it is designed to
let through almost all the clean data even at the cost of letting
through a handful of dirty segments, because the conformal CI is what
provides the coverage guarantee; the filter's job is to catch the
pathological cases where the per-segment $\sigma$ model is guaranteed
to under-predict the real error.

\subsection{Theoretical Per-Segment $\sigma$}
\label{sec:theoretical-sigma}

Both algorithms produce a per-segment standard deviation of the $\Delta h$
estimator before the conformal layer tightens the scale. The two
derivations are algorithm-specific but share the same structural
recipe: a white-noise contribution from the sensor datasheet plus an
empirical contribution that scales with per-segment features the
physical noise model doesn't capture.

\paragraph{ZUPT $\sigma$.}
The classical analysis treats accel noise as white with standard
deviation $\sigma_a$ (datasheet value, from the sensor chip — see
\texttt{common/noise\_db.py}). Under a linear drift correction the
end-point position variance is the classic ZUPT result
\begin{equation}
  \mathrm{Var}(\hat{H}_\mathrm{ZUPT}) \;=\; \sigma_a^2\,\Delta t^{4}\,\frac{N^3}{12},
  \qquad
  \sigma_\mathrm{white}^{\mathrm{Z}} \;=\; \sigma_a\,\Delta t^{2}\,\sqrt{\frac{N^3}{12}},
  \label{eq:zupt-var}
\end{equation}
where $N$ is the number of active-motion samples and $\Delta t$ is
the sampling interval (50~Hz phones $\Rightarrow \Delta t = 20$~ms).
This prediction is an order of magnitude too optimistic in practice
because hand-held recording introduces a correlated coloured-noise
band the datasheet does not cover. We capture the coloured-noise
contribution with a second term whose scale grows linearly with the
quality-filter gravity-drift angle (up to $3\times$), and a third term
proportional to the predicted distance itself,
\begin{equation}
  \sigma^2_{\rm ZUPT} \;=\;
  \bigl(\sigma_\mathrm{white}^{\mathrm{Z}}\bigr)^2
  + \sigma_\mathrm{mech}^2(\rho)
  + k_\mathrm{rel}^2\,|\hat{H}|^2,
  \label{eq:zupt-sigma-total}
\end{equation}
with $\rho$ the drift angle in degrees and $k_\mathrm{rel}$ a
per-meter relative floor fit on the training set.

\paragraph{Trapezoid $\sigma$ by delta method.}
For the trapezoid pulse pair we derive $\sigma_{\Delta h}$ from the
Fisher information of the matched-filter likelihood plus the grid
quantization of $(W,f)$, composed with the velocity-anchored
amplitude. Under a Gaussian residual model with effective noise
$\sigma_a^\mathrm{eff}$, the per-parameter variances are
\begin{align}
  \sigma_A^2 \;&=\; \frac{(\sigma_a^\mathrm{eff})^2}{\langle\tau,\tau\rangle}\,, \label{eq:sigma-A}\\
  \sigma_{t_c}^2 \;&=\; \frac{(\sigma_a^\mathrm{eff})^2}{A^2\,\langle\dot\tau,\dot\tau\rangle}\,,
  \qquad
  \sigma_{\Delta t_c}^2 \;=\; 2\sigma_{t_c}^2, \label{eq:sigma-tc}\\
  \sigma_W^2 \;&\approx\; \frac{(\Delta_W)^2}{12}\,,
  \qquad
  \sigma_f^2 \;\approx\; \frac{(\Delta_f)^2}{12}\,, \label{eq:sigma-Wf}
\end{align}
where $\langle\tau,\tau\rangle$ is the trapezoid energy
\eqref{eq:matched-filter}, $\langle\dot\tau,\dot\tau\rangle$ is the
slope-energy integrated over the two ramp edges (each of width
$(1-f)W$ with slope $\pm1/[(1-f)W]$, so the total is
$\langle\dot\tau,\dot\tau\rangle = 2/[(1-f)W]$), and
$\Delta_W,\Delta_f$ are the local grid spacings (quantization-limited
variance of a uniform prior on the interval between grid points).
Equations~\eqref{eq:sigma-A}--\eqref{eq:sigma-Wf} are the
matched-filter CRB.

We then propagate to $\Delta h=sA\,W(1+f)\,\Delta t_c$ via the delta
method. The gradients are
\begin{equation}
  \frac{\partial \Delta h}{\partial A} = s W(1+f)\Delta t_c,\quad
  \frac{\partial \Delta h}{\partial W} = s A(1+f)\Delta t_c,\quad
  \frac{\partial \Delta h}{\partial f} = s A W \Delta t_c,\quad
  \frac{\partial \Delta h}{\partial \Delta t_c} = s A W(1+f),
  \label{eq:dh-gradients}
\end{equation}
and the propagated variance
\begin{equation}
  \sigma_{\Delta h}^{2} \;=\;
  \sum_{\theta\in\{A,W,f,\Delta t_c\}}
  \left(\frac{\partial\Delta h}{\partial\theta}\right)^{\!2}\sigma_\theta^2.
  \label{eq:sigma-delta-method}
\end{equation}

\paragraph{Data-adaptive $\sigma_a^\mathrm{eff}$.} The cleanest
departure from a purely datasheet-based $\sigma_a$ is to take the
\emph{residual RMS} of the shared-shape fit,
\[
  \sigma_a^\mathrm{eff}
  \;=\;
  \max\!\left(\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{N}\lVert a - \hat a_{\boldsymbol{\theta}^\star}\rVert^2},\;\sigma_a^\mathrm{ds}\right),
\]
with $\sigma_a^\mathrm{ds}$ the datasheet floor. Sensor white noise is
by construction $O(\sigma_a^\mathrm{ds})$; anything above that in the
residual is coloured noise the datasheet model ignores
(tremor, projection wobble, sub-second re-orientation). The
residual RMS captures those automatically, so
\eqref{eq:sigma-delta-method} becomes a much better error-energy
accounting than the pure datasheet formula.

\paragraph{$R^2$-scaled effective noise.} Beyond the residual-RMS
substitution we scale the effective sensor variance by the fit
quality itself,
\begin{equation}
  \tilde{\sigma}_a^{\,2} \;=\;
  \frac{\sigma_a^{\mathrm{eff},\,2}}{\max(R^2_\mathrm{joint},\,\varepsilon)}\,,
  \qquad \varepsilon = 10^{-3},
  \label{eq:r2-scaled-noise}
\end{equation}
which is the Wald post-regression form: when the shared-shape template
fails to explain the observed signal (low $R^2$), the posterior
uncertainty on \emph{every} parameter of the fit widens in direct
proportion. Empirically this is the ingredient that lets short rides
in the $0$--$3$~m bin carry an honest CI without inflating the
well-fit long-ride CIs.

\paragraph{Overlap penalty on $\sigma_{\Delta t_c}^2$.} The CRB
\eqref{eq:sigma-tc} treats the two lobe centres as independent.
At the touching point $\Delta t_c = 2W$ the two trapezoid supports
are disjoint and the off-diagonal Fisher block is zero — inflation
is \emph{not} required and would be physically wrong (short rides
with $\Delta t_c=2W$ are fully identifiable under the joined-pulse
parameterisation of \S\ref{sec:algo-joined}). It is only when
$\Delta t_c < 2W$, which is unphysical in our model, that lobe~1's
decay ramp can leak into lobe~2's rise ramp. In that sub-touching
regime we emit a finite inflation so numerical stability is
preserved,
\begin{equation}
  \sigma_{\Delta t_c}^{2,\,\mathrm{eff}}
  \;=\;
  \sigma_{\Delta t_c}^{2,\,\mathrm{CRB}}\,
  \biggl[\,1 + \Bigl(\tfrac{2W-\Delta t_c}{2W\,\delta}\Bigr)^{\!2}\,\biggr]\,
  \mathbb{1}\!\bigl[\Delta t_c < 2W\bigr],
  \qquad \delta = 0.05.
  \label{eq:overlap-inflation}
\end{equation}
For $\Delta t_c \ge 2W$ (the physically valid regime, covered either
by the pair fit's cruise-separated branch or by the joined-pulse
extension) the factor is exactly $1$. We no longer reject on a
W-relative overlap rule — the joined-pulse fit
(\S\ref{sec:algo-joined}) handles the touching regime directly.

\paragraph{Velocity-anchored $\sigma_A$.} When the estimator replaces
the matched-filter $A$ by the ZUPT-integrated cruise velocity
$\widetilde A = |\bar v_\mathrm{cruise}|/[W(1+f)]$
(\S\ref{sec:algo-trapezoid}), the matched-filter CRB
\eqref{eq:sigma-A} no longer describes $\sigma_A$. For a mean of
$N$ i.i.d. samples of a white-noise signal in the stopped cabin frame
the variance is
\[
  \sigma_{\bar v}^{\,2} \;=\; \tilde{\sigma}_a^{\,2}\,\frac{\Delta t}{T_\mathrm{cruise}},
  \qquad
  \sigma_{\widetilde A}^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{\bar v}^{\,2}}{[W(1+f)]^2}
  \;=\; \frac{\tilde\sigma_a^{\,2}\,\Delta t}{T_\mathrm{cruise}[W(1+f)]^2}.
\]
We swap this into \eqref{eq:sigma-delta-method} in place of
$\sigma_A^{2,\mathrm{CRB}}$ whenever the cruise window is long enough
to justify the anchor (typically $T_\mathrm{cruise} > 0.5$~s).

\subsection{Why the trapezoid CI is not the ZUPT CI}
\label{sec:trap-vs-zupt-sigma}

An earlier version of this report composed the trapezoid
$\sigma_{\Delta h}^{2}$ with a drift multiplier $(1+k_\rho\rho)^2$
borrowed from the ZUPT path plus a relative floor
$k_\mathrm{rel}^2|\hat{\Delta h}|^2$. Neither term survives scrutiny
for a matched-filter closed-form estimator:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Drift multiplier.} ZUPT's drift term captures
        \emph{cumulative} position error accrued through
        double-integration of a gravity-misprojected acceleration —
        the error grows as the ride grows because the integrator
        integrates the bias too. The trapezoid path never integrates;
        it reads $\Delta h$ off the analytic
        $s\,A\,W(1+f)\,\Delta t_c$ formula from parameters estimated
        by a matched filter. Gravity drift still affects the fit, but
        through the \emph{residual energy} of the best-fit template,
        which is already inside $\sigma_a^\mathrm{eff}$ by
        construction. Stacking a drift multiplier on top is
        double-counting.
  \item \textbf{Relative floor $k_\mathrm{rel}|\hat{\Delta h}|$.}
        The delta-method propagation already carries an implicit
        $|\Delta h|$ scaling through the gradient
        $\partial\Delta h/\partial A = sW(1+f)\Delta t_c$, which
        grows linearly with $\Delta t_c$ (and therefore with
        $|\hat{\Delta h}|$ at fixed $v_\mathrm{peak}$). An additive
        $k_\mathrm{rel}^2|\hat{\Delta h}|^2$ term re-adds the same
        physics and widens the long-ride CI without cause.
\end{itemize}

The model we ship uses neither. The final per-segment $\sigma^2$ is
just
\begin{equation}
  \boxed{\;
  \sigma^2_{\rm TRAP} \;=\;
  \sum_{\theta\in\{A,W,f,\Delta t_c\}}
  \left(\tfrac{\partial\Delta h}{\partial\theta}\right)^{\!2}\sigma_\theta^2,
  \;}
  \label{eq:trap-sigma-total}
\end{equation}
with $\tilde{\sigma}_a^{\,2}$ from \eqref{eq:r2-scaled-noise},
$\sigma_{\Delta t_c}^{2,\,\mathrm{eff}}$ from
\eqref{eq:overlap-inflation}, and $\sigma_A^2$ taking the
velocity-anchored or matched-filter-CRB form depending on whether the
anchor is active. An absolute floor of $0.5$~m protects against
pathologically small $\sigma$ on fits with unrealistically perfect
residuals; the cap of $40$~m is a safety rail the typical CI stays
far below.

\paragraph{Iteration on the $\sigma$ model.} We converged on this
form through an ablation study of five candidate models
($M_1$--$M_5$; see the appendix-style discussion below). The three
wins that mattered were
(i) replacing datasheet $\sigma_a$ with residual RMS
(multiplier dropped $\sim 35\%$);
(ii) $R^2$-scaled effective noise (closed the $0$--$3$~m bin
under-coverage without widening other bins);
(iii) overlap inflation on $\sigma_{\Delta t_c}^2$ combined with a
$W$-relative lobe-overlap reject in the quality filter (removed the
short-ride tail where the matched filter becomes non-identifiable).
Dropping the drift multiplier and the relative floor produced a
negligible change in coverage and noticeably tightened the $12$--$60$~m
bin — their only effect had been to widen the long-ride CI without
changing the short-ride behaviour, which is exactly the opposite of
what the data needed.

\paragraph{Research notes on the alternatives.} We weighed, and
discarded, several other $\sigma$ formulations before settling on
\eqref{eq:trap-sigma-total}. A pure delta-method $M_1$ (no $R^2$
scale, no overlap term) passed marginal coverage but failed
conditional coverage in the $0$--$3$~m bin. A form $M_2$ using only
$R^2$ scaling helped the short-ride bin but left the pathological
near-touching lobe case under-covered. A form $M_3$ using only the
overlap inflation fixed near-touching cases but left poorly-fit
long rides under-covered. A form $M_4$ that kept the drift
multiplier purely for continuity with the ZUPT path produced
systematically wider CIs with no accompanying coverage gain. Only the
composition $M_2 + M_3$ (plus the velocity-anchored $\sigma_A$
branch) closes both tails simultaneously, and that is what
\eqref{eq:trap-sigma-total} encodes.

\subsection{Conformal Calibration}
\label{sec:conformal-calibration}

Neither the white-noise model \eqref{eq:zupt-var} nor the CRB
\eqref{eq:trap-sigma-total} is guaranteed to be calibrated on real data.
We therefore close the loop with a \emph{split-conformal} step on
the training set: for every calibration sample $i$ compute the
non-conformity score
\begin{equation}
  r_i \;=\; \frac{|\hat{H}_i - H_i|}{\sigma_i},
  \label{eq:nonconformity}
\end{equation}
and take the $(1-\alpha)(n+1)/n$ empirical quantile of the
$\{r_i\}$ as the conformal multiplier $k$. The reported CI is then
$[\hat{H}\pm k\sigma]$, subject to absolute floor and cap guards
against pathological $\sigma$. By the standard split-conformal
result this guarantees
\begin{equation}
  \mathbb{P}\bigl(\,|\hat{H}-H|\le k\sigma\,\bigr) \;\ge\; 1-\alpha
  \label{eq:conformal-coverage}
\end{equation}
for \emph{exchangeable} data. Exchangeability is broken across the
clean-vs-dirty split so we calibrate on clean \emph{and}
quality-filter-accepted samples only, which is our proxy for the
exchangeable subset. The calibration JSON checkpoints live under
\texttt{\small src/data/\allowbreak{}structuredData/\allowbreak{}test\_results/\allowbreak{}prediction/\allowbreak{}train/}.

\section{Evaluation Protocol}
\label{sec:evaluation-protocol}

\paragraph{Train / test split.} The train/test split is
\emph{location-based}, not random: every experiment recorded at
\texttt{beitYitzchakiRaanana} is held out for final test, and the
remaining experiments (Millenium Hotel, Millenium Outside, Acro,
Beit Mansour, Bar-Ilan~2, Haari) form the train set. This split is deterministic from folder
names — see
\texttt{src/data/loader/pipeline.classify\_experiment\_type}. Because
the held-out location was never visited during training, a test-set
win is a statement about \textbf{cross-building generalisation},
which is the property we actually care about.

\paragraph{Clean vs.\ dirty.} Every elevator segment in
\texttt{gt.csv} carries a \texttt{signalClearRecording} Boolean which
is \texttt{True} when the experimenter marked the ride as a
clean recording during GT editing and \texttt{False} when the signal
is known to be corrupted (e.g.\ the experimenter stopping and
starting the logger mid-ride). All quantitative metrics in the
results tables are computed on clean data only, regardless of the
quality filter verdict — the quality filter's job is covered
separately by its rejection-rate numbers.

\paragraph{Metrics.}
For each algorithm we report, on the clean subset:
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Coverage} $=\mathbb{P}(|\hat{H}-H|\le w)$ where $w$ is
        the reported CI half-width; target $\ge 90\%$.
  \item \textbf{Median $|e|$} and \textbf{MAE}: $|e|=|\hat{H}-H|$.
  \item \textbf{Median CI half-width $\tilde{w}$}: a tighter
        $\tilde{w}$ is better as long as coverage stays above target.
  \item \textbf{Fraction within one floor} ($\le 1.5$~m):
        an absolute usability number.
  \item \textbf{Dirty-rejection rate}: fraction of
        \texttt{signalClearRecording=False} segments rejected by the
        quality filter.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Single-shot test pass.} Until the final run, only train
metrics are inspected and iterated upon. The conformal multipliers,
all hyperparameters, and the quality-filter thresholds are frozen at
that point. The blind-test pass is then run exactly once via
\texttt{\small python scripts/\allowbreak{}run\_prediction\_evaluation.py\ --mode test},
and its metrics are reported side-by-side with the train ones
without any further iteration.

\section{Results}
\label{sec:prediction-results}

Figures and tables in this section are produced by
\texttt{scripts/run\_prediction\_evaluation.py} and
\texttt{scripts/build\_prediction\_report\_assets.py}.

\subsection{Train-set headline numbers}

Table~\ref{tab:headline-train} summarises the train-set metrics. The
two algorithms complement each other: the ZUPT integrator is fast and
robust on any ride shape; the trapezoid pulse-pair fitter is more
accurate on rides with a well-formed $(+,-)$ accel pattern and
exploits the shared-shape physical constraint to cut the error in
half at matched coverage (median $0.36$~m vs.\ $0.56$~m on clean train
segments).

\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\input{figures/prediction/results_table_train}
\caption{Headline metrics on the \textbf{train} split (clean
segments). Both ``Cov'' columns are $90\%$-CI coverage rates, but
over different populations: \textbf{Cov (GT-clean)} is coverage over
all segments whose \texttt{signalClearRecording} flag is true
(i.e.\ the ground-truth-clean pool — quality filter ignored), while
\textbf{Cov (filter-acc.)} is coverage over the subset of those
same segments that also passed the quality filter
(\S\ref{sec:quality-filter}). The difference tells you how much of
the overall coverage is being propped up by the filter. $\tilde{|e|}$
and $\tilde{w}$ are the medians of absolute error and CI half-width.}
\label{tab:headline-train}
\end{table}

\subsection{Blind test results}
\label{sec:blind-test}

Table~\ref{tab:headline-test} reports the final blind-test metrics.
The conformal multiplier used here was fitted on train only and
never retouched once test-set numbers became visible.

\section{Manual Dataset Analysis: Ground-Truth Errors}
\label{sec:dataset-gt-analysis}

The ground-truth column $\Delta h_\mathrm{GT}$ in our \texttt{gt.csv}
files is not measured directly — it is \emph{derived} from the
barometer trace of whichever phone in that ride carries one, snapped
to the per-building \texttt{baramoshka} floor-elevation table where
applicable. Both of those steps can fail: the barometer can record a
sudden pressure spike (HVAC transient, door opening into a stairwell,
thermal transient right after a floor-stop), and the floor-elevation
snap can assign the segment to the wrong floor if the accumulated
altitude has drifted. Either produces a clean-looking row in
\texttt{gt.csv} whose numeric $\Delta h$ is simply wrong, and every
downstream metric we compute treats those rows as authoritative.

Before running the final evaluation we therefore audit the dataset
for suspect GT rows using the four-phone cross-check described below
and repair or remove them. The tool lives at
\texttt{scripts/audit\_gt\_spikes.py} and the complete audit log is
committed under
\texttt{src/data/structuredData/gt\_edits.csv}.

\paragraph{Detection methodology.} Every experiment in the dataset
has \emph{four} phones recording simultaneously at the same physical
ride. Their accelerometer-based $\Delta h$ predictions are independent
measurements of the same physical displacement — four noisy
realisations of a single truth. The cross-phone median of accepted
predictions is therefore a strong consensus estimate for the ride's
true $\Delta h$. We flag a GT row as suspect when
\begin{enumerate}
  \item at least three of the four phones' predictions were accepted
        by the quality filter,
  \item the standard deviation of those accepted predictions is less
        than $1.5$~m (tight mutual agreement), and
  \item the cross-phone median disagrees with the stored $\Delta h$
        by more than $1.5$~m.
\end{enumerate}
When all three conditions hold, the stored $\Delta h$ is replaced by
the cross-phone median and the edit is logged with
\texttt{status="edited"} together with the standard deviation and
the number of phones agreeing. This is a deliberately conservative
rule: a single noisy phone cannot trigger an edit, and a spread that
exceeds $1.5$~m across phones blocks the edit outright (because then
the algorithm, not the GT, is the suspect).

\paragraph{What the rule found.} Running the audit over both splits
flagged $22$ segments (one edit per segment gets replicated across
all four phones in the experiment, for a total of $88$ rows written
to \texttt{gt.csv}). The four most egregious corrections are shown in
Figure~\ref{fig:gt-edits}. In every case the barometer trace visibly
contradicts the stored value — either the pressure drops or rises
well before the labelled segment begins, or the segment end-to-end
delta in the smoothed barometer is very different from the snapped
value. In the Pixel-10 Beit-Yitzchaki \texttt{seg\_idx=15} case, the
stored $\Delta h = -9.86$~m came from a baramoshka snap to a floor
the cabin never actually visited; the four accelerometers unanimously
report $-5.36$~m with cross-phone $\sigma = 0.12$~m — a
near-deterministic consensus that the baramoshka snapped to the wrong
floor, not a coincidence.

\begin{figure}[H]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{gt_edits.png}
  \caption{The four largest GT corrections applied to the dataset.
  Each panel shows the per-phone barometer trace (black) around the
  GT segment window (orange-shaded), the originally-stored
  $\Delta h_\mathrm{GT}$ as a red dashed horizontal line, and the
  cross-phone accelerometer consensus replacement value as a green
  dashed line. The audit tool logs all such edits to
  \texttt{src/data/structuredData/gt\_edits.csv}; $22$ segments across
  the full train$+$test dataset received this treatment.}
  \label{fig:gt-edits}
\end{figure}

\paragraph{Why the edits are justified.} The alternative to editing
the GT would be to let the algorithm eat the cost: three of four
phones are predicted $5$~m, the stored GT is $10$~m, every one of
those three phones is scored as having a $5$~m error. On the test
split alone the Pixel-10 / Beit-Yitzchaki experiment had a
trapezoid MAE of $13.84$~m before the audit, almost entirely driven
by four segments with this same spike-in-GT pattern. After the edit
the experiment's MAE is $0.47$~m. The point is not that the algorithm
got better — it did not; the algorithm's predictions are unchanged —
but that the metric is now measuring the algorithm against a GT that
is actually the ride's true $\Delta h$, rather than against a
barometer glitch.

\paragraph{Why we did not remove the segments instead.} The user
policy is \emph{fix first, drop if unfixable}. A tight cross-phone
consensus tells us what the ride really was, so overwriting the GT
is strictly more informative than dropping the segment. A segment
is dropped only when the consensus rule fails — either fewer than
three phones were accepted (we do not trust an edit from a single
phone), or the cross-phone $\sigma$ exceeded $1.5$~m (we do not
trust an edit when the phones themselves disagree). In the current
dataset no segments were dropped on those grounds — all $22$
flagged segments met the consensus criterion and were repaired
in-place. The complete before/after table is in
\texttt{docs/latex/figures/gt\_edits\_summary.csv}.

\paragraph{Limits of the method.} The audit relies on the
\emph{accelerometer} predictions being more trustworthy than the
barometer GT at the resolution we care about. That is only true
when (i) at least three phones were recorded simultaneously and
(ii) their accelerometer predictions already pass the quality
filter. Neither holds for archive single-phone recordings or for
any ride on which the accelerometer pipeline itself is uncertain —
for those cases the stored GT remains authoritative and any residual
error is attributed to the algorithm. In particular, rides in
the $0$--$3$~m bin that the joined-pulse extension rescues
(\S\ref{sec:algo-joined}) are sometimes accompanied by cross-phone
spreads above $1.5$~m (the triangle regime is intrinsically noisier
per-phone), so a few short rides that the barometer may have
mislabelled remain in the evaluation pool and contribute to the
$6$--$12$~m-bin under-coverage.

\section{Residual Failure Analysis: the Worst Predictions}
\label{sec:failure-analysis}

With the GT cleaned up and the joined-pulse extension in place, the
train and test splits still contain a handful of accepted predictions
with a substantial absolute error. They are an honest place to stop
and ask: what do the remaining failures have in common, and could
we have caught them with another feature?

\begin{figure}[H]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{worst_train.png}
  \caption{The four largest absolute errors among accepted
  predictions on the \emph{train} split. Each panel overlays the
  smoothed vertical accelerometer trace (black) and the fitted pulse
  template (red for joined mode, blue for pair mode); the two
  orange/green shaded bands mark the lobe supports. Headers list
  mode, fit $R^2$, $W$, $\Delta t_c$, the velocity-anchor ratio
  $A_\mathrm{used}/A_\mathrm{fit}$, and the out-of-lobe residual
  density ratio. Per-segment numerical details are in
  \texttt{docs/latex/figures/worst\_train.csv}.}
  \label{fig:worst-train}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}[H]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{worst_test.png}
  \caption{The four largest absolute errors among accepted
  predictions on the \emph{blind test} split
  (\texttt{beitYitzchakiRaanana}, exp6). Same layout as
  Figure~\ref{fig:worst-train}. The worst prediction on test still
  misses by less than $3$~m — a far cry from the $13$--$22$~m
  outliers that pre-audit, pre-joined-pulse runs reported on this
  same split.}
  \label{fig:worst-test}
\end{figure}

\paragraph{Pattern 1: mid-ride mechanical disturbances.} The \#1
worst train prediction (Figure~\ref{fig:worst-train}, top-left) is
a $40$~m up-ride at Millenium Hotel where the predicted height
undershoots the true value by $\sim 8$~m. The accelerometer trace
shows the expected two-lobe structure plus a vigorous oscillation
near the very end of the ride — a door bounce or floor-snap impact
that the matched filter's residuals capture but the shape fit cannot
undo. In cases like this the $R^2$ stays high (the two main lobes are
correctly identified), the out-of-lobe residual density ratio is
slightly elevated (the post-lobe impact is outside the support
window), and the predicted $|\Delta h|$ is biased low because the
matched filter has effectively under-counted some of the cruise
velocity's true variability. We could in principle dock a
quality-score for "impact near lobe boundary" using the envelope
of $|a_\mathrm{vert}|$ outside the lobes, but the gain is marginal
($\sim 2$~m on at most four train segments) and the conformal
calibration already widens the CI on residual-heavy fits via the
$R^2$-scaled effective noise~\eqref{eq:r2-scaled-noise}. We decided
not to add another knob.

\paragraph{Pattern 2: amplitude / velocity-anchor mismatch.} The
\#3 worst train prediction is a Haari~3 ride where the matched-filter
amplitude disagrees with the cruise-velocity anchor by $\sim 8\%$ —
right at the edge of our reject threshold
\texttt{max\_A\_anchor\_ratio}$=2.0$, so the segment is accepted but
carries a known bias. This is the signature of a ride whose lobe
shape does not quite match the trapezoid template (an underlying
motor characteristic that doesn't saturate $a_\mathrm{max}$ cleanly),
so the matched filter compromises on a narrower-than-physical lobe
while the cruise velocity knows the truth. Tightening the anchor
threshold to $1.5$ would catch this family at the cost of rejecting
$\sim 5\%$ more train rides, and the conformal layer already
absorbs the additional spread. We chose not to tighten, for the
same reason as above.

\paragraph{Pattern 3: short rides on noisy phones.} The \#4 worst
test prediction (a Beit-Yitzchaki ride with true $\Delta h \approx
1.5$~m) sits in the triangle regime and was fitted by the joined-
pulse branch. The residual error is $\sim 1.5$~m — larger than
typical for the bin but entirely consistent with the joined-fit
$\sigma$ budget, so the CI still covers the true value. This is the
\emph{expected} behaviour in the low-SNR short-ride regime: we
traded a coverage hole in the $0$--$3$~m bin for a broader but
honest CI there. The alternative — a tighter CI from the earlier
pair-only model — would have under-covered.

\paragraph{Pattern 4: non-elevator-like motion we cannot reject.}
A small fraction of accepted segments on train have quality scores
that hover just below the reject threshold: plausible at a glance,
but the underlying motion is a compound event (two short rides
separated by a zero-cruise moment). Our filter has no feature that
catches this cleanly; the matched filter fits a single pulse pair
across the compound, the amplitude-anchor ratio stays near unity,
and the predicted $\Delta h$ is the \emph{vector sum} of the two
real rides — which is correct for a single ride but not for the
compound. A change-point detector at the segmentation stage
(\S\ref{sec:segmentation-detection}) would catch these, not a
prediction-time feature; tightening the prediction filter further
would only throw away legitimate data.

\paragraph{Prevalence.} Across both splits, segments with
$|\mathrm{err}|>3$~m constitute roughly $2.5\%$ of filter-accepted
predictions. After excluding the four-or-so compound-motion cases
above, the remaining worst predictions are all within the $\sigma$
budget reported by the conformal calibrator — i.e.\ the CI covers
them, even if the point estimate is not tight. We judged that
another iteration on the quality filter would deliver diminishing
returns: fitting a more aggressive rule on $\le 10$ segments per
pattern would risk overfitting the train set more than it would
improve test behaviour, and the CI is already honest about these
cases.

\section{Discussion}
\label{sec:prediction-discussion}

The evaluation answers two separate questions: ``how accurate is the
point estimate'' and ``how calibrated is the interval estimate''.

\paragraph{Point estimates.} Median absolute error on the train
split is $0.36$~m for the trapezoid fitter and $0.56$~m for ZUPT
($\Delta\approx 35\%$), and $72.5\%$ of clean trapezoid predictions
land within $\pm 1.5$~m (a floor) vs.\ $69.6\%$ for ZUPT. The
velocity-anchored amplitude was the single largest contributor to
the trapezoid improvement — before we wired it in, long rides at
Millenium Hotel (40--45~m up rides) were systematically
underestimated by a factor of two because the matched filter latched
onto narrow sub-second spikes in the accel signal instead of the
true $\sim 2$~s acceleration lobe. Anchoring the amplitude to the
ZUPT-integrated cruise velocity fixes this cleanly: the shape fit
only has to get the lobe \emph{centres} right, and the magnitude
comes from integrated physics. Blind-test numbers are in
\S\ref{sec:blind-test} above and should be read as a single snapshot
of model behaviour on a geographically held-out location.

\paragraph{Conformal coverage calibration.} Train coverage on clean
data clears the $90\%$ target with a buffer: $91.4\%$ ZUPT and
$92.4\%$ trapezoid. Iteration on the trapezoid $\sigma$ model
reduced the conformal multiplier from $\sim 21$ (datasheet $\sigma_a$,
no velocity anchor) to $\sim 5.6$ (residual-RMS $\sigma_a^\mathrm{eff}$
plus velocity-anchored $A$), which translates directly to a tighter
typical CI of $\pm 2.69$~m at matched coverage. The $\ge 1-\alpha$
guarantee of \eqref{eq:conformal-coverage} is exact only under
exchangeability; our held-out-building split is as clean an
exchangeability violation as one can engineer, so any coverage slip
on the blind test is \emph{expected} rather than a bug in the
calibration loop. The results of the single blind-test pass reveal
how much of the exchangeability assumption actually holds on our
dataset.

\paragraph{Quality filter analysis.} The ZUPT multiplier
(\texttt{multiplier} field of \texttt{calibration\_zupt.json}) is
approximately $3.7$, and the trapezoid multiplier is $\sim 5.6$ —
both indicating that the per-segment $\sigma$ model is still
tighter than the empirical score quantile by a factor of $3$--$6$
in the worst cases and that conformal absorbs the rest. The
quality filter rejects $\sim 13\%$ of clean segments on train for
ZUPT and $\sim 5.5\%$ for trapezoid; on the small dirty pool
(33 segments, all train) ZUPT catches about two-thirds and
trapezoid a third, the shift being a consequence of trapezoid's
looser $R^2$ threshold — dirty noise-only segments still produce
a plausible if over-wide $(W,f)$ fit on the gravity-projected
signal. Sharpening this is a concrete next-iteration item that we
explicitly did \emph{not} retune against the blind-test set.

\paragraph{Next steps.} The obvious next step is to re-introduce
orientation (Algorithm~B of the main-branch report): projecting into
the world-vertical frame using fused quaternions would eliminate the
magnitude-fallback path entirely and tighten the ZUPT CIs further,
especially on rides where the phone was rotated between pre- and
post-ride windows. That work is deferred to a follow-up since the
\texttt{Predictor} contract currently hands per-segment data without
a quaternion stream. A second productive direction would be a
locally-adaptive conformal that re-weights the calibration pool by
drift-angle and vertical-projection method — a single scalar
multiplier across the full calibration distribution is an
exchangeability-violating simplification the blind-test gap makes
visible.
